title: REPUBLIC OF KENYA
text:
REPUBLIC OF KENYA
IN THE TAX APPEALS TRIBUNAL AT NAIROBI
TAT APPEAL NO. E763 OF 2025
ROCHE KENYA LIMITED.............….............................................
…....……APPELLANT
-VS-
COMMISSIONER OF CUSTOMS & BORDER CONTROL...................….
RESPONDENT
JUDGMENT
BACKGROUND
1. The Appellant is a private limited company incorporated in Kenya and
whose business is the promotion, marketing, and distribution of
diabetic care, diagnostic products, and pharmaceutical products.
2. The Respondent is the principal officer appointed under Section 13 of
the Kenya Revenue Authority Act. The Kenya Revenue Authority is an
agency of the Government of Kenya mandated with the duty of
collecting and receipting all tax revenue, and the administration and
enforcement of all tax laws set out in Parts 1 & 2 of the First Schedule
to the Act, for the purposes of assessing, collecting, and accounting for
all tax revenues in accordance with those laws.
3. On 18th September 2024, the Respondent issued the Appellant a Notice
of Post Clearance Audit for the period from September 2019 to
September 2024.
Judgment TAT No. E763 of 2025 - Roche Kenya Ltd -vs- Commissioner of Customs
& Border Control Page 1 of 23

4. On 28th March 2025, the Respondent issued the Appellant a
Management Letter and Notice of Demand, demanding short-levied
duties of Kshs. 2,215,212.00 on account of undervaluation and tariff
misclassification.
5. On 25th April 2025, the Appellant lodged its application for review,
challenging the Respondent’s demand pursuant to Section 229 of the
East Africa Community Customs Management Act (EACCMA).
6. On 22nd May 2025, the Respondent issued its Review Decision, partially
allowing the Appellant’s review application.
7. Aggrieved by the Respondent’s Review Decision, the Appellant lodged
this Appeal vide its Notice of Appeal dated 4th July 2025.
THE APPEAL
8. In its Memorandum of Appeal dated and filed on 18th July 2025, the
Appellant raised the following grounds of appeal: -
a. The Respondent erred in law and fact by failing to fully
appreciate the commercial and contractual context underpinning
the determination of the import value of Herceptin.
b. The Respondent erred in law and in fact by seeking to disregard
the use of the Transaction Value (TV) method, which the
Appellant applied in declaring its customs value of Herceptin.
c. The Respondent erred in law and in fact in concluding that
imported immunological products are not classifiable under tariff
code 3002.15.00, but rather under 3006.93.00; and
Judgment TAT No. E763 of 2025 - Roche Kenya Ltd -vs- Commissioner of Customs
& Border Control Page 2 of 23

d. The Respondent erred in law and in fact by concluding that
imported items of tariff codes 3402.42.00, 3822.19.00 and
3808.99.90 ought to be classifiable under 3402.90.00
APPELLANT’S CASE
9. The Appellant case is based on its Statement of Facts dated and filed
on 18th July 2025, and its Written Submissions filed on 12th February
2026.
10. The Appellant averred that the Respondent’s decision to uplift
the customs value, as observed in paragraph 2.1 of the Review
Decision, was based on values reflected in the 2022 price list provided
by the Appellant, which had the price of Herceptin listed as USD 364.
11. The Appellant further averred that the Respondent relied on
inconsistencies between the declared unit prices and the delivery price
to justify the adjustment in relation to Herceptin. In doing so, the
Respondent disregarded the Appellant’s explanation that the pricing
structure was influenced by a negotiated public health arrangement
with the Ministry of Health (MoH) aimed at improving access to cancer
treatment.
12. The Appellant asserted that in reaching its conclusion to reject
the Transaction Value (TV) method, the Respondent failed to consider
legitimate commercial and contractual factors that influenced pricing,
such as the public health agreement with the MoH, internal transfer
pricing policies, and communication.
13. The Appellant stated that it entered a Memorandum of
Understanding (MoU) with the MoH on 20th June 2022, which was valid
Judgment TAT No. E763 of 2025 - Roche Kenya Ltd -vs- Commissioner of Customs
& Border Control Page 3 of 23

for a period of five years. The MoU created a partnership centered on
creating access for breast cancer patients while reducing the cost
burden and improving the health outcomes for affected patients in
Kenya, with particular emphasis on public health facilities.
14. It is the Appellant’s case that the MoU with the MoH had a
broader objective of establishing an integrated care model for the
prevention, diagnosis, and management of breast, non-Hodgkin
lymphomas, and colorectal cancers at national and primary care levels.
15. The Appellant contended that prior to entering an MoU with the
MoH, public health facilities would buy Herceptin through local
distributors. As an example, the Appellant provided an invoice of a
local sale to one such distributor, Surgipharm Limited, which indicated
a price of Kshs. 69,000 per unit of Herceptin. This demonstrated that
the price to public health facilities that would buy from the distributor,
at the second commercial level, was quite high. At this time, the Free
on Board (FOB) value from Roche Basel from January 2022 was USD
364.
16. The Appellant further contended that after the coming into force
of the MoU with the MoH, the Appellant notified Roche Basel of the fact
and sought an appropriate transfer price as reflected in internal group
email communications dated 12th July 2022.
17. The Appellant stated that in January 2023, the transfer price from
Roche Basel was revised upward to USD 217 following a group-wide
transfer pricing review. The transfer prices reflected in the Appellant’s
price lists are typically in line with Clause 6 of the Distribution
Agreement between the Appellant and Roche Basel. The clause
prescribes that the purchase price of pharmaceutical products will be
Judgment TAT No. E763 of 2025 - Roche Kenya Ltd -vs- Commissioner of Customs
& Border Control Page 4 of 23

subject to periodic review to ensure that the transactions between the
Appellant and Roche Basel are at arm’s length. Despite this adjustment
in FOB price, the price offered by KEMSA remained unchanged as
anticipated in the MoU and as evidenced by three invoices issued to
KEMSA for Herceptin in March, July, and December 2023.
18. The Appellant asserted that the price of pharmaceutical products
is volatile in nature; hence, there need to undertake annual reviews. It
is a known fact that the price of pharmaceutical products is influenced
by various factors, including the economic purchasing power in the
market. It is on this basis that Roche Basel adjusted the sales price of
Herceptin to make the drug more accessible while managing its
internal transfer pricing requirements. This reduction in price would
enable the transactions with the Appellant to remain at arm’s length
while, at the same time, supporting the MoH in the fight against breast
cancer.
19. The Appellant maintained that the variations in pricing are
justified and supported by documentation, including price lists, internal
communications, and the MoU. The decision by the Respondent to
uplift the customs value of Herceptin based on the 2022 price list was,
therefore, unjustified and did not reflect the actual commercial realities
of the transaction.
20. The Appellant stated that the customs entry uplifted by the
Respondent in relation to Herceptin was entry number
22NBOIM409156620 dated 12th September 2022. The entry pertains to
an import made after the MoU with the MoH came into effect on 20th
June 2022, and after the revised transfer price of USD 137 per unit was
implemented on 15th July 2022. The validity of the revised price is
further supported by the invoice from Roche Basel to the Appellant,
Judgment TAT No. E763 of 2025 - Roche Kenya Ltd -vs- Commissioner of Customs
& Border Control Page 5 of 23

dated 1st September 2022, and therefore reflected the updated pricing
structure and should not have been uplifted by the Respondent.
21. On the Respondent’s disregard of the Transaction Value (TV)
method, the Appellant stated that in paragraph 2.1 of the Review
Decision, the Respondent rejected the Appellant’s declared TV of USD
137 per unit of Herceptin, despite the Appellant’s reliance on a MoU
with the MoH to justify the pricing. As evidenced in the internal group
email communication dated 12th July 2022, the Appellant notified its
parent company, Roche Basel, of the MoU and requested a revised
transfer price. The price was subsequently adjusted to USD 137 per
unit effective 15th July 2022. The mid-year change was a direct result of
the MoU and was implemented accordingly.
22. The Appellant asserted that the Respondent instead adopted a
higher unit price of USD 364 from a 2022 delivery price list provided
during the audit, without demonstrating that the conditions for
rejecting the TV under the EACCMA had been met. Therefore,
effectively disregarding the TV method as the primary basis of customs
valuation was contrary to the legal framework.
23. The Appellant asserted that it correctly applied the TV at the
time of making the customs declarations for the Herceptin in line with
the provisions of Section 122(1) of the EACCMA, which provides that
“where imported goods are liable to import duty ad valorem,
then the value of such goods shall be determined in
accordance with the Fourth Schedule and import duty shall be
paid on the value.”
24. It is the Appellant’s case that paragraph 2(1) further sets out
four primary conditions for the use of the TV method as follows: -
Judgment TAT No. E763 of 2025 - Roche Kenya Ltd -vs- Commissioner of Customs
& Border Control Page 6 of 23

(a)there are no restrictions as to the disposition or use of the goods
by the buyer other than restrictions which:
i. are imposed or required by law or by the public
authorities in the Partner State;
ii. limit the geographical area in which the goods may be
resold; or
iii. do not substantially affect the value of the goods;
(b)the sale or price is not subject to some condition or
consideration for which a value cannot be determined with
respect to the goods being valued;
(c)No part of the proceeds of any subsequent resale, disposal,
or use of the goods by the buyer will accrue directly or
indirectly to the seller unless an appropriate adjustment
can be made in accordance with the provisions of
Paragraph 9; and
(d)the buyer or seller is not related, or where the buyer and
seller are related, that the transaction value is acceptable
for customs purposes under the provisions of
subparagraph (2)”
25. It is the Appellant’s case that it complied with all four conditions
stated in paragraph 2(1) for the use of the TV method as laid out
below: -
a. The suppliers have not imposed any restrictions as to how the
Appellant may use or dispose of the goods that it purchases from
it;
b. There are no other conditions or considerations imposed by the
suppliers with respect to the sale or price charged by the
Appellant other than the ones foreseen in paragraph 2(1) of the
Fourth Schedule to EACCMA;
Judgment TAT No. E763 of 2025 - Roche Kenya Ltd -vs- Commissioner of Customs
& Border Control Page 7 of 23

c. Disposal proceeds from the subsequent resale of products by the
Appellant did not accrue directly or indirectly to the suppliers;
and
d. The relationship between the Appellant and Roche Basel did not
influence the price of the imported items, as the price is charged
on an arm’s length basis.
26. The Appellant asserted that according to the Notes’ Fourth
Schedule, the TV method is the primary method for valuation, and
imported goods are to be valued in accordance with the method
whenever the conditions prescribed under it are fulfilled. Further, it is
only when the customs value cannot be determined using the TV
method that other methods of valuation can be applied in sequential
order.
27. The Appellant further asserted that it declared the value of the
goods invoiced by the Appellant’s sellers in accordance with the TV
guidelines in the EACCMA. The Respondent, therefore, should have
vacated its decision to uplift the customs values of the imported items,
as none of the exceptional circumstances contained in paragraph 2(1)
of the Fourth Schedule of the EACCMA that prohibit the use of the TV
method are applicable.
28. The Appellant contended that in paragraph 2.2 of the Review
Decision, the Respondent concluded that certain imported
immunological products were incorrectly classified under tariff code
3002.15.00, and instead reclassified them under 3006.93.00 as
placebos.
29. In its objection to this ground, the Appellant further contended
that the Respondent, in its findings, relied on invoice descriptions
Judgment TAT No. E763 of 2025 - Roche Kenya Ltd -vs- Commissioner of Customs
& Border Control Page 8 of 23

indicating the products were for clinical trials and noted that some
consignments had previously been declared under 3006.93.00.
However, the Respondent failed to distinguish between active
immunological substances used in investigational research and inert
placebos. By treating all such imports as placebos, the Respondent
overlooked the nature and function of the active pharmaceutical
ingredients, thereby misapplying the tariff classification.
30. The Appellant asserted that the products in question, although
described in some invoices as “placebos” were in fact biologically
active immunological agents used in clinical research. These products
were not inert substances but contained pharmacologically active
ingredients intended for use in investigational settings.
31. The Appellant stated that the term “placebo” is well-defined in
both local and international regulatory contexts. The Pharmacy and
Poisons Board (PPB) of Kenya, in its Guidelines for the Conduct of
Clinical Trials, defines a placebo as “an active substance or treatment
(inert substances with no pharmacologic activity) that looks the same
as, and is given in the same way as, an active drug or
intervention/treatment being studied.”
32. It is the Appellant’s case that Tariff Code 3002.15.00 of the EAC
Common External Tariff structure 2022 (EAC CET 2022) covers
“immunological products, put up in measured doses or in forms or
packing for retail sale.” The heading includes monoclonal antibodies
and other biotechnologically derived substances used for therapeutic,
prophylactic, or diagnostic purposes, including those used in clinical
trials.
Judgment TAT No. E763 of 2025 - Roche Kenya Ltd -vs- Commissioner of Customs
& Border Control Page 9 of 23

33. The Appellant argued that the Respondent’s failure to consider
the scientific and regulatory context of the imported goods led to a
misapplication of the tariff code 3006.93.00, which is narrowly
intended for placebos and blinded clinical trial kits.
34. The Appellant further argued that the Respondent’s reference to
prior declarations under 3006.93.00 does not justify reclassification.
Classification must be determined based on the specific facts and
documentation of each importation. The principle of consistency
cannot override the requirement for accurate classification based on
the goods’ objective characteristics.
35. Thus, the Appellant asserted that its imported products were
correctly classified under tariff code 3002.15.00, and the Respondent’s
reclassification to 3006.93.00 was both factually and legally flawed.
36. In its final ground of appeal, the Appellant stated that at
paragraph 2.2 of the Review Decision, the Respondent contended that
certain imported items declared under tariff codes 3822.19.00, and
3808.99.90 were misclassified and instead ought to fall under tariff
code 3402.90.00.
37. The Appellant asserted that it is the Respondent’s position that
the goods were cleaning preparations and therefore fell under the
broader category of organic surface-active agents. However, the
Respondent failed to consider the specific nature, formulation, and
intended use of the products, which are diagnostic reagents and
maintenance solutions used in laboratory and clinical analyzers.
38. The Appellant submitted that the products in question are not
general-purpose cleaning agents but are specialized reagents designed
Judgment TAT No. E763 of 2025 - Roche Kenya Ltd -vs- Commissioner of Customs
& Border Control Page 10 of 23

for use in diagnostic equipment. These products are essential for
maintaining the accuracy and integrity of laboratory test results by
preventing contamination and ensuring the proper functioning of the
analyzers. Their classification must therefore reflect their diagnostic
function, not merely their cleaning properties.
39. The Appellant reiterated that the descriptions of the products
under headings 3822.19.00 and 3808.99.90 establish that the products
are not general-purpose cleaning agents but are integral to the
operation of diagnostic analyzers. Therefore, the classification is
appropriate as these headings cover diagnostic reagents and
disinfectants used in clinical settings.
40. The Appellant affirmed the above position in its written
submissions dated 12th February 2026, and placed reliance on
precedents set in Galot vs. Kenya Revenue Authority
(Commercial Appeal E006 of 2021) (2024) KEHC 12486 (KLR),
Rohto Mentholatum (Kenya) Ltd vs KRA ((2023) KETA 271; and
Cadbury Kenya Ltd vs Commissioner of Customs & Border
Control (2023) KETA 951 (KLR), amongst others.
Appellant’s Prayers
41. The Appellant prayed to the Tribunal for the following orders: -
a) The Appeal be allowed with costs to the Appellant.
b) The Respondent’s Review Decision dated 22nd May 2025 be set
aside in its entirety.
c) Any other orders that the Tribunal may deem fit.
Judgment TAT No. E763 of 2025 - Roche Kenya Ltd -vs- Commissioner of Customs
& Border Control Page 11 of 23

RESPONDENT'S CASE
42. The Respondent filed its Statement of Facts dated 21st August
2025 in opposition to the Appeal. The Respondent also filed its written
submissions on 23rd February 2026.
43. It is the Respondent’s case that the dispute herein relates to a
demand for short-levied taxes issued upon the Appellant on 28th March
2025 regarding the Appellant’s undervaluation of Herceptin vial 5ml
600mg together with tariff misclassification of both placebos and
clearing solution.
44. The Respondent averred that the Appellant undervalued the
customs value for the product Herceptin vial 5ml 600mg 1 by relying
on the unit price of USD 137 instead of the unit price of USD 364. The
Appellant also wrongly classified placebo and cleaning solutions under
tariff codes 3002.15.00 and 3808 respectively.
45. The Respondent further averred that it properly appreciated the
commercial and contractual context underpinning the determination of
the import value of Herceptin, and nothing in the MoU between the
Appellant and MoH provided for any pricing arrangement for the
Appellant’s import to allow the Appellant to apply a lower value for
customs purposes.
46. It is the Respondent’s case that it noted that the Appellant had a
distribution agreement with its foreign supplier, Hoffmann La Roche
Ltd (Roche Basel). In the said agreement, the purchase price of the
imported products would be determined periodically by the foreign
supplier to enable the Appellant achieve its operating margins.
Judgment TAT No. E763 of 2025 - Roche Kenya Ltd -vs- Commissioner of Customs
& Border Control Page 12 of 23

47. The Respondent asserted that it relied on the “Delivery Price
List” that had been provided by the Appellant during the audit, in
which it was noted that the declared value of some imported items was
different from those agreed upon in the agreement.
48. The Respondent stated it deemed the price to be the actual price
payable for customs purposes, and as such, the items found to be
declared with lower figures compared to what was on the delivery
price list were uplifted.
49. The Respondent averred that pursuant to the 4th Schedule of the
EACCMA, the customs value of imported goods is the transaction
value, which is the price actually paid or payable for the goods.
50. The Respondent further averred that the Appellant disregarded
the delivery price of the product known as Herceptin, which, according
to the documents provided, was to be effective from 1st January 2022
until further notice.
51. It is the Respondent’s case that it was guided by Section 122(4)
of EACCMA 2004 as read with the 4th Schedule, which provides that the
customs value of imported goods shall be the transaction value, which
is the price actually paid or payable for the goods when sold for export.
52. According to the Respondent, it deemed the delivery price as
the actual payable value, as the same was the price actually paid by
the Appellant during importation.
53. The Respondent averred that from the price lists, Herceptin vial
5ml 600mg 1 was assigned a price of USD 364 per unit for 2022 and
USD 217 per unit on both delivery price list dated 1st January 2023 and
15th March 2023 respectively.
Judgment TAT No. E763 of 2025 - Roche Kenya Ltd -vs- Commissioner of Customs
& Border Control Page 13 of 23

54. The Respondent further averred that it correctly classified
immunological products under tariff code 3006.93.00, instead of the
Appellant’s classification under 3002.15.00, as the Appellant’s invoices
indicated that the products were for clinical trial use only, and as such,
there is a clear distinction in the description when the import is the
active drug. When it is a placebo, the descriptions clearly state that the
products are placebos to the specific drugs.
55. The Respondent asserted that there have been instances where
the Appellant had imported and properly classified the placebos under
code 3006.93.00 in several consignments. Thus, cleaning
solutions/preparations were correctly reclassified under tariff code
3402.90.00.
56. The Respondent averred that the Appellant’s imports are
cleaning solutions designed to clean diagnostic systems or analyzers.
The Appellant instead classified acid wash as reagents under heading
3822, which provides for diagnostic or laboratory reagents.
57. The Respondent stated that the Appellant also declared Sys
Wash Elecsys Cobas under heading 3808, which provides for
insecticides, rodenticides, fungicides, herbicides, ant sprouting
products, and plant growth regulators. However, from the description
and the product data sheets of the items imported by the Appellant,
the products were not reagents or insecticides or similar products, but
clearing agents classifiable under tariff code 3402.90.00.
58. The Respondent averred that all actions were taken in
accordance with Section 234, 235 and 236 of the EACCM Act, 2004
when it conducted the audit upon the Appellant.
Judgment TAT No. E763 of 2025 - Roche Kenya Ltd -vs- Commissioner of Customs
& Border Control Page 14 of 23

59. The Respondent averred that, as per the entry made in the
customs declaration, the amount invoiced by the Appellant was
different from the delivery price list, and no further evidence was
provided by the Appellant to prove the deviation from the price list and
the customs entry.
60. The Respondent posited that no evidence has been provided,
including payments made to the exporters, to prove that indeed the
same amounts entered in the customs entry were the same amounts
paid to the exporter.
61. The Respondent asserted that it observed from the Appellant’s
invoices presented that the products were for clinical trial use only,
and as such, there is a clear distinction in the description when the
import is the active drug. The Appellant failed to demonstrate why it
deviated from the agreed-upon price as per the agreement and
delivery prices and decided to apply a lower value.
62. In its written submissions dated 23rd February 2026, the
Respondent affirmed the above arguments and placed reliance on the
precedent established in Fidelity & Commercial Bank Ltd -vs-
Kenya Grange Vehicle Industries Ltd (2017) eKLR to assert that
the Appellant cannot rely on an agreement which expressly does not
mention the reduction of value for customs purposes to indicate that
the same gave it carte blanche powers to reduce its value.
Respondent’s Prayers
63. The Respondent prayed to the Tribunal for the following orders:
a) The Appeal be dismissed with costs to the Respondent.
Judgment TAT No. E763 of 2025 - Roche Kenya Ltd -vs- Commissioner of Customs
& Border Control Page 15 of 23

b) The Respondent’s Review Decision dated 22nd May, 2025 be
upheld.
ISSUES FOR DETERMINATION
64. The Tribunal, having considered the parties' pleadings,
submissions, and documents filed before it, is of the view that the
issue that falls for its determination is whether the Respondent’s
Review Decision dated 22nd May, 2025 is justified.
ANALYSIS AND DETERMINATION
65. This Appeal was triggered by the Respondent’s Review Decision
dated 22nd May, 2025. In the said Review Decision, the Respondent
partially allowed the Appellant’s review application and upheld its
additional assessment of the Appellant of Kshs. 1,910,281.00 on
account of import undervaluation and tariff misclassifications.
66. In particular, the Respondent upheld its tax assessment of Kshs.
1,105,722.00 on account of the undervaluation of customs value for
the product Herceptin vial 5ML 600mg 1; Kshs. 465,375.00 on account
of tariff misclassification of placebos; and Kshs. 339,185.00 on account
of tariff misclassification of cleaning solutions.
Customs Undervaluation of Herceptin vial 5ML 600mg 1
67. On customs undervaluation of the product Herceptin vial 5ML
600mg 1, the Respondent justified its decision that the Appellant
availed an MoU between it and the MoH, which the Appellant alleged
was the basis for negotiating the unit price. However, according to the
Respondent, the MoU concerned is an agreement on how the products
Judgment TAT No. E763 of 2025 - Roche Kenya Ltd -vs- Commissioner of Customs
& Border Control Page 16 of 23

imported by the Appellant will be made accessible to patients in Kenya
once KEMSA purchases and distributes them.
68. The Respondent further justified its decision on the basis that the
delivery price list for 2022 availed by the Appellant under the name
Herceptin vial 5ML 600mg 1 was assigned a price of USD 364 per unit,
and no information was provided by the Appellant to confirm that the
MoU was used to negotiate the price downwards to USD 137 per unit.
The Respondent asserted that further, as per the delivery price lists
dated 1st January 2023 and 15th March 2023 availed during the audit,
the unit price of the product was USD 217 in both cases.
69. On its part, the Appellant submitted that the Respondent relied
on inconsistencies between the declared unit prices and delivery price
lists to justify the adjustment in relation to Herceptin. In so doing, the
Respondent disregarded the Appellant’s submission that the pricing
structure was influenced by a negotiated public health agreement with
the MoH aimed at improving access to cancer treatment.
70. The Appellant further submitted that the Respondent’s decision
to uplift the customs value of Herceptin, as observed in paragraph 2.1
of the Review Decision, is inherently flawed given that the pricing of
Herceptin is singular in nature, in that the pricing structure was
influenced by a negotiated public health arrangement with the MoH
aimed at improving access to cancer treatment.
71. It is the Appellant’s submission that in reaching its conclusion to
reject the Transaction Value (TV), the Respondent failed to consider
legitimate commercial and contractual factors that influenced pricing,
such as the public health agreement with the MoH, internal transfer
pricing policies, and communication.
Judgment TAT No. E763 of 2025 - Roche Kenya Ltd -vs- Commissioner of Customs
& Border Control Page 17 of 23

72. The Tribunal has considered rival submissions on the
undervaluation of the Appellant’s imported product, Herceptin vial 5ML
600mg 1. From the record before the Tribunal, it is apparent that the
Appellant entered an MoU with MoH regarding enhancing access to
cancer treatment that was in force during the impugned period of the
alleged undervaluation.
73. It is equally apparent that in upholding the unit price of USD
364, the Respondent its customs valuation based on the values of the
2022 price list. From the record before it, the Tribunal, however, notes
that the valuation of Herceptin vial 500ML 600mg relates to
consignment entry number 22NBOIM409156620 dated 12th September
2022. This customs entry was imported during the pendency of the
MoU with MoH and has a Transaction Value (TV) of USD 137, and
further supported by relevant invoices to Roche Basel on record.
Indeed, there is also email correspondence on record requesting the
new pricing as well.
74. Section 122(1) of EACCMA provides as follows regarding the
application of the Transaction Value (TV) method:
“Where imported goods are liable to import duty ad
valorem, then the value of such goods shall be
determined in accordance with the Fourth Schedule,
and import duty shall be paid on that value”
75. Paragraph 2 of the Fourth Schedule of EACCMA provides as
follows:
“The customs value of imported goods shall be the
transaction value, which is the price actually paid or
Judgment TAT No. E763 of 2025 - Roche Kenya Ltd -vs- Commissioner of Customs
& Border Control Page 18 of 23

payable for the goods when sold for export to the
Partner State...”
76. The utility of the Transaction Value (TV) method as the primary
basis for customs valuation has been affirmed by the High Court in
Galot vs. Kenya Revenue Authority (Commercial appeal E006 of
2011) (2024) KEHC 12486 (KLR), and the Tribunal finds no basis for
distinguishing this decision in the instant Appeal.
77. Accordingly, the Tribunal finds that the Respondent was not
justified in disregarding the transaction value of the product Herceptin
vial 500ML 600mg 1 supported by invoices on record. The
Respondent’s additional tax assessment of the Appellant on this basis
is thus not justified.
Tariff Classification of Placebos
78. On the classification of placebos, the Respondent justified its
decision on the ground that the invoices presented stated that the
products were for clinical trials use only, and there is a clear distinction
in the description when the import is the active drug. When it is a
placebo, the descriptions clearly state that the product is “placebos to
the specific drugs.” The Respondent further stated that there are
several consignments in which the Appellant declared placebos
correctly under tariff code 3006.93.00.
79. In rebuttal, the Appellant submitted that the Respondent was
grossly misguided as to the nature of the products, given that the
Respondent failed to distinguish between active immunological
substances used in investigational research and inert placebos. Thus,
by treating all such imports as placebos, the Respondent overlooked
Judgment TAT No. E763 of 2025 - Roche Kenya Ltd -vs- Commissioner of Customs
& Border Control Page 19 of 23

the nature and function of the active pharmaceutical ingredients,
thereby misapplying the tariff classification.
80. The Appellant further submitted that the products in question,
although described in some invoices as “placebos,” were, in fact,
biologically active immunological agents used in clinical research. The
products were not inert substances but contained pharmacologically
active ingredients intended for use in investigational settings.
81. The Appellant further sought to rely on the definition of
“placebo” in the Pharmacy and Poisons Board (PPB) of Kenya
Guidelines for the Conduct of Clinical Trials, which defines it as “an
inactive substance or treatment (inert substances with no
pharmacologic activity) that looks the same as, and is given
the same way as, an active drug or intervention/treatment
being studied.”
82. The Tribunal notes that the record before it does not include the
entry details of the specific consignment classified as “placebos.” The
Appellant, however, has conceded that the products in question were
described in some of the invoices as “placebos.” The said invoices
have not been placed on record before the Tribunal and therefore, the
Tribunal has not had the benefit of reviewing the descriptive aspects of
the consignment and products therein.
83. Tariff Code 3006.93.00 of the EACCMA covers “placebos and
blinded (or double-blinded) clinical trial kits for a recognized clinical
trial, put up in measured doses.” The Appellant asserted that it
imported active pharmaceutical products belonging to tariff code
3002.15.00, which covers “immunological products, put up in
measured doses or in forms or packings for retail sale.”
Judgment TAT No. E763 of 2025 - Roche Kenya Ltd -vs- Commissioner of Customs
& Border Control Page 20 of 23

84. The above rival averments and submissions do not discharge
the Appellant of its burden to prove, with specificity, the details of its
imported products and avail supporting documents. This is supported
by Sections 56 and 59 of the Tax Procedures Act (cap 469B) and
Section 30 the Tax Appeals Tribunal Act (cap 469A). In the instant
appeal, this obligation has not been discharged by the Appellant at this
stage, and the Tribunal finds no basis to disturb the Respondent’s
reclassification of the Appellant’s consignment under tariff code
3006.93.00. The Respondent’s additional tax assessment on this
account is thus justified.
Tariff Classification of Cleaning Solutions
85. On the classification of Cleaning Solutions, the Respondent
submitted that they are classifiable under tariff code 3402.20.00 or
3402.90.00 which provides for “organic surface-active agents (other
than soap); surface-active preparations, washing preparations
(including auxiliary washing preparations) and cleaning preparations,
whether or not containing soap, other than those of heading 34.01”
86. On its part, the Appellant submitted that the goods were
cleaning preparations and therefore fell under the broader category of
organic surface-active agents. Thus, the Respondent’s failure to
consider the specific nature, formulation, and intended use of the
products, which were diagnostic reagents and maintenance solutions
used in laboratory and clinical analyzers, was incorrect in both fact and
law.
87. As already observed above, the Tribunal notes that the record
before it does not include the entry details of the specific consignment
classified as “cleaning solutions.” The said specific consignment details
Judgment TAT No. E763 of 2025 - Roche Kenya Ltd -vs- Commissioner of Customs
& Border Control Page 21 of 23

would have formed a strong basis for reviewing the descriptive and
proprietary content of the impugned products that informed the rival
classifications by the Appellant and the Respondent.
88. The Tribunal affirms that the above rival averments and
submissions, of themselves, do not discharge the Appellant of its
burden to prove, with specificity, the details of its imported products
and avail supporting documents in accordance with Sections 56 and 59
of the Tax Procedures Act (cap 469B) and Section 30 the Tax Appeals
Tribunal Act (cap 469A). This obligation has not been discharged by the
Appellant at this stage, and the Tribunal finds no basis to disturb the
Respondent’s reclassification of the Appellant’s consignment under
tariff code 3402.90.00. The Respondent’s additional tax assessment on
this account is thus also justified.
FINAL DISPOSITION
89. Accordingly, the instant appeal partially succeeds, and the
Tribunal proceeds to make the following orders: -
a. The Appeal be and is hereby partially allowed.
b. The Respondent’s Review Decision dated 22nd May, 2025 be and
is hereby varied in the following terms: -
i. The Respondent’s additional assessment regarding customs
undervaluation is hereby set aside.
ii. The Respondent’s additional assessments regarding the
reclassification of placebos and cleaning solutions are
hereby upheld.
Judgment TAT No. E763 of 2025 - Roche Kenya Ltd -vs- Commissioner of Customs
& Border Control Page 22 of 23

c. Each Party is to bear its costs.
90. It is so ordered.
